attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the early pregnancy clinic at eight weeks gestation in her first pregnancy. She is concerned about her history of genital herpes, which was first diagnosed two years ago. She has experienced approximately three outbreaks per year since then, with the most recent episode four months ago. She has no other medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. She is particularly anxious about the risk to her baby and whether she will require a Caesarean section.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are no current genital lesions. Type-specific herpes simplex virus serology confirms herpes simplex virus type 2 infection.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding delivery?
options: Vaginal delivery is contraindicated regardless of lesion status
Caesarean section is required if lesions occur at any time during pregnancy
Vaginal delivery is appropriate unless active lesions are present in labour
Weekly cervical swabs from 36 weeks gestation are required
attributes.explanation:
This patient has established recurrent genital herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection, which has different implications for pregnancy compared to primary infection. The risk of neonatal HSV infection is highest (30-50%) when primary infection occurs near term but very low (<1%) in women with recurrent infection.

Vaginal delivery is appropriate unless active lesions are present in labour is the correct management approach for women with recurrent genital herpes. The presence of active lesions at the time of labour is the key determinant for delivery method.

Elective Caesarean section is required is incorrect as this would be overly interventionist for recurrent HSV. Caesarean section should be reserved for cases where active lesions are present during labour.

Weekly cervical swabs from 36 weeks gestation are not necessary in the absence of symptoms. However, suppressive antiviral therapy (acyclovir or valaciclovir) from 36 weeks gestation is recommended to reduce the risk of recurrence at delivery.

Vaginal delivery is contraindicated regardless of lesion status represents an overly cautious approach that is not supported by evidence. The presence of active lesions, not the history of HSV itself, determines the need for Caesarean section.

Caesarean section is required if lesions occur at any time during pregnancy is incorrect. Only lesions present during labour are relevant to the decision regarding mode of delivery. Lesions earlier in pregnancy do not necessitate Caesarean section if they have resolved by the time of labour.
learningPoints: Caesarean section is only indicated for women with active genital herpes lesions at the time of labour.
Recurrent genital herpes during pregnancy has a very low risk of neonatal transmission.
Suppressive antiviral therapy from 36 weeks gestation reduces the risk of lesions at delivery in women with recurrent herpes.
references: Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases, Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Management of perinatal infections [Internet]. Melbourne (VIC): RANZCOG; [cited 2024 Dec 6]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/ASID-Management-of-Perinatal-Infections.pdf
